description: This figure presents a three-panel mechanical-property chart showing the effect of manganese content (24–34 wt%) on an Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel containing fixed 9% Al and 0.9% C, water quenched from 1050°C and aged at 550°C for 16 h. The top panel plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid triangles) and 0.2% yield strength (YS, open triangles) versus Mn content: UTS peaks near 1200 MPa at 26 wt% Mn and remains above 1100 MPa up to approximately 32 wt% Mn before falling to approximately 950 MPa at 34 wt% Mn, while 0.2% YS stays near 1000 MPa between 26 and 28 wt% Mn. The middle panel shows ductility indicators reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, solid circles); ψ reaches approximately 60% at 30 wt% Mn and δ peaks near 45% at 30–31 wt% Mn, both declining sharply beyond 32 wt% Mn. The bottom panel gives U-notch impact toughness K_CU (open diamonds), which remains relatively stable at 100–115 J/cm² from 24 to 32 wt% Mn but collapses to approximately 35 J/cm² at 34 wt% Mn. The caption states that the degradation at high Mn is attributed to beta-Mn precipitation during aging, and the accompanying text reports that increasing Mn raises the solubility of Al and C in austenite, thereby reducing the amount of strengthening kappa-phase that precipitates during aging.
